blocks.statement: I can solve a multi-hill roller-coaster problem using ME conservation and find a pendulum's speed at the bottom from its release angle.

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ in an elliptical orbit IS a roller coaster — KE and PE trade off periodically. Faster at perihelion (low U), slower at aphelion (high U).
blocks.connection: Same equation, different scale. The bookkeeping is identical.

blocks.markdown:
## Hills and swings — same bookkeeping

On a frictionless coaster, **ME = KE + PE stays constant** at every point on the track. That gives a shortcut that skips kinematics entirely:

**½v² + gh = constant** (the mass cancels — every cart, heavy or light, gets the same speeds)

Starting from rest at height h₁, the speed at any other height h is:

**v = √(2g·(h₁ − h))**

Only the DROP matters — not the shape of the track between.

A pendulum is the same problem in disguise. The only new step is geometry: a bob on a string of length L released at angle θ from vertical starts at height

**h = L(1 − cosθ)**

above its lowest point. From there it's the same bookkeeping: mgh = ½mv² at the bottom.

terms.definition: For a pendulum of length L released from angle θ, the bob's height above its lowest point is h = L − L·cosθ = L(1 − cosθ). A 1-m pendulum released at 60° above vertical: h = 1·(1 − cos60°) = 1·(1 − 0.5) = 0.5 m.
terms.cognate: Sp. altura de péndulo · Pt. altura do pêndulo

blocks.title: The height is L(1 − cosθ), not L
blocks.markdown: Don't use the string length L as the height. The bob only rises h = L(1 − cosθ) above its lowest point — at 60°, that's exactly HALF the string length.

blocks.prompt: THREE-HILL ROLLER COASTER. A coaster cart starts from rest at the top of hill 1 (h₁ = 30 m). Frictionless. Find its speed at the top of hill 2 (h₂ = 18 m) and hill 3 (h₃ = 22 m).
blocks.given: v₁ = 0 · h₁ = 30 m · h₂ = 18 m · h₃ = 22 m
blocks.equation: ME₁ = ME₂ = ME₃ → ½v² + gh = ½v₁² + gh₁ = gh₁
blocks.answer: v₂ ≈ 15.3 m/s, v₃ ≈ 12.5 m/s. Higher hill → slower cart.

blocks.prompt: PENDULUM RELEASED AT 60°. A 1-kg pendulum bob on a 1-m string is released from θ = 60° above vertical. Find the bob's speed at the bottom of its swing.
blocks.given: m = 1 kg · L = 1 m · θ = 60° · friction-free
blocks.equation: h = L(1 − cosθ) AND mgh = ½mv²
blocks.answer: ≈ 3.13 m/s at the bottom of the swing.

blocks.title: Orbits are roller coasters
blocks.markdown: An asteroid in elliptical orbit: KE and PE trade. Faster near the Sun, slower far away. Same physics as a roller coaster — just on cosmic scale.

blocks.instruction: Draw the three-hill coaster (h₁ = 30 m, h₂ = 18 m, h₃ = 22 m) and the pendulum released at 60°. Label the heights that matter.
prompts: On the coaster, mark the DROP h₁ − h₂ — that difference is what sets v₂.
On the pendulum, show h = L(1 − cosθ), NOT the string length L.
Put a speed arrow where each object moves fastest.

blocks.prompt: COASTER BOTTOM FROM 40 M. A coaster cart starts from rest at the top of a 40-m hill. Frictionless. What's its speed at the bottom?
Check your 40-m coaster answer, then complete the pendulum frame.
blocks.frame: A pendulum at angle θ has height h = ___ above its lowest point. The bottom speed is v = ___.
